sources: Tim Warner O'Reilly course plan July 2026

tips.title: AI Credits replaced premium requests on June 1, 2026
tips.description: Premium requests are legacy. Billing is usage-based on GitHub AI Credits at $0.01 each, metered on input, output, and cached tokens at API rates. Code completions and Next Edit Suggestions stay free and consume no credits. Open the Usage panel in VS Code or the CLI to watch burn in real time.

tips.title: Code completions and NES stay free after the switch
tips.description: Inline completions and Next Edit Suggestions remain free across all paid plans after June 1, 2026. Lean on completions and NES for high-frequency edits and reserve premium models for chat, agent, and review work where the cost is justified.

tips.title: Copilot code review consumes GitHub Actions minutes
tips.description: Since June 1, 2026 Copilot code review draws against your GitHub Actions minute pool on top of AI Credits. On monorepos, scope review-on-PR with path filters rather than enabling it for every pull request.

tips.title: Watch the multiplier table for Opus 4.7
tips.description: Claude Opus 4.7 jumps from a 7.5x to a 27x multiplier on annual plans during the credits transition. If your team relies on Opus for deep reasoning, model the new burn rate against the published multiplier table before renewal.

tips.title: Use the in-IDE usage panel to track spend
tips.description: VS Code 1.115+ and the Copilot CLI both surface a live usage panel showing requests, tokens, and credits. Pin it during a heavy agent session so a runaway loop does not silently blow the budget.

tips.title: Default to GPT-5 mini for everyday work
tips.description: GPT-5 mini is the included model and consumes no AI Credits. Make it your default in the model picker for routine completions, refactors, and small chats; switch to a premium model only when you actually need the extra reasoning depth.

tips.title: Reach for Claude Opus 4.7 on hard reasoning tasks
tips.description: Opus 4.7 is the strongest reasoning model in the Copilot lineup as of July 2026. Use it for architectural design, complex refactors, and tricky bug hunts; it is Pro+, Business, and Enterprise only since the Pro tier removal in April 2026.

tips.title: Use GPT-5.3-Codex for agentic coding
tips.description: GPT-5.3-Codex is roughly 25 percent faster than 5.2-Codex on agentic tasks and is GA as of February 9, 2026. Business and Enterprise admins must enable it via policy. Pick it when you want autonomous file edits and terminal commands.

tips.title: Audit pinned models after every deprecation wave
tips.description: GitHub retires models steadily, and a prompt file or agent file pinned to a removed model fails or silently falls back. Claude Sonnet 4 went in May 2026 and GPT-5.2 is gone as well. Grep .github/prompts, .github/agents, and CI workflows for hard-coded model names each time a deprecation lands.

tips.title: Treat the model picker like a cost dial
tips.description: Match model to task: Haiku 4.5 for quick rewrites, Sonnet 5 or GPT-5.4 for balanced work, Opus 4.8 or GPT-5.6 for deep reasoning. Under AI Credits the price difference is token-metered, so switching mid-conversation is the cheapest optimization you can make. Auto model selection picks for you unless you pin.

tips.title: Pick the cloud agent's model from github.com
tips.description: Since April 14, 2026 you can choose Claude or Codex models for cloud agent sessions launched from github.com. Pick the model that matches the task before you delegate, instead of accepting the default.

tips.title: Use a model-array fallback in custom agents
tips.description: In a .agent.md file, set model to an array such as ["claude-opus-4-7", "gpt-5.5"]. Copilot tries them in order, so a quota lockout on Opus rolls over to GPT-5.5 instead of failing the agent run.

tips.title: Use Agent mode for autonomous multi-step tasks
tips.description: Agent mode lets Copilot pick which files to alter, propose code and terminal commands, and iterate until done. Trigger it with Ctrl+Shift+I or by switching the chat to Agent. Keep prompts outcome-shaped: describe the destination, not the keystrokes.

tips.title: Steer the agent mid-task instead of restarting
tips.description: While the agent is running you can drop in a follow-up like 'Use the existing ErrorHandler utility instead of writing custom try/catch blocks.' Steering is much cheaper than rerunning the whole task with a longer prompt.

tips.title: Use Plan mode before risky agent runs
tips.description: Plan mode produces a structured implementation plan before any file edits. Read the plan, edit it inline if needed, then approve. It is the cheapest way to keep an agent from rewriting half your repo.

tips.title: Try Autopilot mode for hands-off sessions
tips.description: Autopilot, in public preview since April 2026, runs an agent without per-step confirmation. Use it on safe, well-scoped tasks (test generation, doc updates) where you are willing to review the diff at the end rather than every step.

tips.title: Configure auto-approval rules for trusted tools
tips.description: Add tools or commands you trust (formatters, test runners, read-only shell commands) to your auto-approval rules so the agent stops asking. Keep destructive commands like rm and force-push off the list.

tips.title: Run long jobs with send_to_terminal
tips.description: send_to_terminal, added April 2026, lets the agent kick off background terminal sessions without blocking the chat. Use it for builds, test suites, and migrations that take more than a minute.

tips.title: Reach for the Debugger agent on stubborn bugs
tips.description: The Debugger agent (April 2026) validates fixes against the live runtime instead of just reading the static code. Use it when a fix passes static review but fails when you actually run the program.

tips.title: Delegate Explore, Task, Plan, and Code Review subagents
tips.description: Agent mode delegates to specialized subagents. Ask explicitly: 'Use the Explore subagent first, then hand off to Plan.' Naming the subagent up front prevents the orchestrator from skipping reconnaissance.

tips.title: Use the cloud agent for background PR work
tips.description: Assign an issue to GitHub Copilot and the cloud agent creates a branch, commits, opens a PR, and writes the description in the background via GitHub Actions. Review the PR like any other; you can request changes and the agent iterates.

tips.title: Write the issue like a brief, not a ticket
tips.description: The cloud agent reads the issue body as its prompt. Include intent, constraints, acceptance criteria, and links to related code. A two-line ticket gets a two-line PR; a thorough brief gets a thorough PR.

tips.title: Launch cloud agent sessions from Visual Studio
tips.description: As of April 2026 you can start a cloud agent session directly from Visual Studio, not just from VS Code or github.com. Useful when your daily IDE is VS but your team uses cloud agents for long-running work.

tips.title: Configure firewall endpoints by plan
tips.description: cloud agent runners use plan-specific endpoints since February 27, 2026: Pro/Pro+ uses api.individual.githubcopilot.com, Business uses api.business.githubcopilot.com, Enterprise uses api.enterprise.githubcopilot.com. Add the right one to your egress allowlist.

tips.title: Cloud agent startup is more than 20 percent faster
tips.description: Custom Actions runner images shipped in April 2026 cut cold-start time by over 20 percent. If you previously avoided the cloud agent because of latency, retest it; the gap to a local agent run is much smaller now.

tips.title: Steer the cloud agent through PR reviews
tips.description: The cloud agent reads PR review comments and reacts to them. Treat the review like a conversation: leave specific, actionable comments instead of a vague 'please refactor this' and the next push will be more focused.

tips.title: Install the Copilot CLI from @github/copilot
tips.description: The terminal-native agent installs with npm install -g @github/copilot, which requires Node 22 or later. The binary is copilot. Do not confuse it with the older gh copilot GitHub CLI extension, which was retired and repurposed as a launcher. Homebrew and WinGet packages are also available.

tips.title: Replay an agent run with /chronicle
tips.description: /chronicle prints a structured timeline of an agent session: prompts, tool calls, file edits, and outcomes. It went generally available in April 2026. Drop it into a debugging session to see exactly what the agent did.

tips.title: Free tokens with /compact
tips.description: /compact summarizes earlier conversation turns into a compact context block. Run it before kicking off a long agent task to keep the working context focused on what matters.

tips.title: Inspect what the agent sees with /context
tips.description: /context shows everything the agent currently has loaded — files, instructions, MCP tools. Use it when answers feel off; nine times out of ten the agent just is not seeing the file you assumed it had.

tips.title: Toggle ACP permission modes mid-session
tips.description: The CLI now supports switching ACP (Agent Context Protocol) permission modes mid-session. Drop into a stricter mode while the agent touches sensitive files, then loosen up for the rest of the run.

tips.title: Chain MCP actions with experimental MCP Tasks
tips.description: MCP Tasks (experimental in CLI v1.0.39+) lets you chain MCP server actions into a multi-step pipeline. Useful for repeatable workflows like 'pull issue, summarize, draft PR' across different MCP servers.

tips.title: Define a custom agent in .github/agents/*.agent.md
tips.description: An agent file is markdown plus YAML frontmatter listing tools, MCP servers, model, and instructions. Specialize agents per concern — readme-creator, refactor-specialist, security-reviewer — and Copilot routes prompts to the closest match.

tips.title: Use AGENTS.md at the repo root for the singular pattern
tips.description: AGENTS.md (singular) at the repo root is the current standard alongside the .github/agents/ directory. Both formats are recognized; AGENTS.md is the right place for the one main agent description.

tips.title: Scope tools to enforce a trust boundary
tips.description: A read-only Code Review agent should not list editFiles in its tools. Tool scope is a security boundary: the agent literally cannot write files if the tool is not granted. Build read-only specialists this way.

tips.title: Author once, run everywhere with SKILL.md
tips.description: As of April 2026, the same SKILL.md file works in GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex CLI without modification. Required frontmatter is name (lowercase-hyphenated, unique) and description; license is optional.

tips.title: Invoke a skill as a slash command
tips.description: Drop a skill at .github/skills/[name]/SKILL.md and it auto-loads when relevant. You can also call it explicitly: '/webapp-testing for the login page.' That deterministic call beats hoping the orchestrator picks the right skill.

tips.title: Use user-level agents for personal workflows
tips.description: Custom agents are now user-level as of April 2026, in addition to repo-level. Ship your personal 'changelog-writer' or 'commit-message-fixer' agent with you across every repo without committing it.

tips.title: Mine the awesome-copilot library for skills
tips.description: github/awesome-copilot is the community library of vetted skills. Browse it before writing a new skill — chances are someone has already published a comparable one you can fork and adjust.

tips.title: Migrate .chatmode.md to .agent.md
tips.description: Legacy .chatmode.md files still load but the .agent.md format is the current standard. Rename and update frontmatter when you next touch the file; do not let a long tail of deprecated files build up.

tips.title: Anchor the project with copilot-instructions.md
tips.description: Drop a .github/copilot-instructions.md describing tech stack, conventions, build commands, and architectural patterns. Every chat request automatically picks it up — quality of suggestions jumps the first day.

tips.title: Use path-scoped instruction files
tips.description: Files under .github/instructions/*.instructions.md with applyTo globs scope guidance to specific paths. Have a powershell.instructions.md that only kicks in for **/*.ps1 and **/*.psm1, separate from your TypeScript guidance.

tips.title: Document build and validation steps for the agent
tips.description: Spell out exact commands for bootstrap, build, test, lint, and run in copilot-instructions.md. Include known quirks and order dependencies. The agent stops guessing and runs your real validation pipeline.

tips.title: Configure commit message and PR description instructions
tips.description: github.copilot.chat.commitMessageGeneration.instructions and pullRequestDescriptionGeneration.instructions let you point at .md files describing your conventions. Conventional Commits, ticket-link format, signed-off-by — all enforced consistently.

tips.title: Use prompt files with the agent: field
tips.description: Reusable prompts live at .github/prompts/*.prompt.md. The mode: field is deprecated; use agent: instead. Variable syntax is ${input:varName}. Tweak once, reuse forever, share across the team.

tips.title: Edit instructions and prompts in the unified editor
tips.description: VS Code 1.115/1.116 introduced a unified chat customization editor — instructions, prompts, and chat modes in a single pane. Use it to spot conflicts (two instruction files contradicting each other) at a glance.

tips.title: Reference existing docs in your instructions
tips.description: Point copilot-instructions.md at PRODUCT.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, and CONTRIBUTING.md. Tell Copilot to flag conflicts when it spots them. Your existing docs become live context without duplication.

tips.title: Extend Copilot with MCP servers
tips.description: Model Context Protocol servers expose external systems — databases, ticketing, custom tools — to agent mode. Configure them in your project settings; agent mode discovers and uses them automatically when the task fits.

tips.title: Use the built-in GitHub MCP server
tips.description: The CLI ships with a GitHub MCP server covering issues, PRs, repos, and search. No setup required — ask the agent about an issue or open a PR and it routes through the MCP server transparently.

tips.title: Authenticate MCP servers with OAuth
tips.description: Slack, Jira, and other third-party MCP servers support OAuth. Use it for secure delegated access instead of pasting tokens into config files. Rotation and revocation become a one-click operation.

tips.title: Allowlist MCP servers at the org level
tips.description: GHEC admins can configure MCP server allowlists for the org. Combine with model availability policy to lock down which external integrations Copilot can touch from inside enterprise codebases.

tips.title: Use streamable HTTP transport when SSE is blocked
tips.description: MCP supports streamable HTTP transport alongside SSE. If your network blocks long-lived SSE connections, switch transports without rewriting the server.

tips.title: Name MCP servers with npm-style identifiers
tips.description: MCP server names accept dots, slashes, and @ characters now. Adopt npm-style scoped names like @acme/jira-mcp to avoid collisions when you publish multiple servers.

tips.title: Spin up persistent context with Spaces
tips.description: GitHub Copilot Spaces (GA September 2025) bundles repos, code, PRs, issues, files, and free-text notes into a persistent context hub. Use Spaces for onboarding, query libraries, and architecture decision records.

tips.title: Share read-only Spaces by link
tips.description: Public Spaces are link-shared and view-only — perfect for handing context to a contractor or a candidate. Org-shared Spaces support admin, editor, and viewer roles for tighter control.

tips.title: Query Spaces from the IDE via MCP
tips.description: The GitHub MCP server exposes Spaces, so any IDE that speaks MCP can query Space content from chat. Use this to pull onboarding notes into a fresh checkout without leaving VS Code.

tips.title: Copilot Memory is public preview, and its clock resets
tips.description: Memory stores repository-level facts and user-level preferences, shared across the cloud agent, code review, and the CLI. It is on by default for individual plans but admin-gated for Business and Enterprise. Entries are removed after 28 days without use; the timer resets each time Copilot successfully uses one.

tips.title: Validate useful memories so they persist
tips.description: When Memory surfaces a useful fact, validate it. Validated memories survive past the 28-day expiry. Treat memory hygiene like dependency updates — a quick weekly sweep keeps it sharp.

tips.title: Enable Copilot for code review on PRs
tips.description: Copilot Code Review uses agentic tool calling for full project context, plus CodeQL, ESLint, and other deterministic tools. Enable it on the repo and tag @copilot in a PR comment to start a review.

tips.title: Hand off Code Review to the cloud agent for fixes
tips.description: When Code Review flags an issue, hand off to the cloud agent to apply the fix. The handoff turns 'here is what is wrong' into 'here is the PR that fixes it' without manual round trips.

tips.title: Codify review guidelines in markdown files
tips.description: Author backend-review-guidelines.md and frontend-review-guidelines.md, then point reviewSelection.instructions at them. Copilot applies the right guidelines for the file path being reviewed instead of generic advice.

tips.title: Get focused review with /review on a selection
tips.description: Select a function, run /review in chat, and Copilot critiques only that scope using your configured review instructions. Faster and more targeted than a full PR review for surgical changes.

tips.title: Pin the right chat mode: Ask, Edit, or Agent
tips.description: Ask is read-only Q&A. Edit makes scoped multi-file changes you approve. Agent runs autonomously. Pick deliberately — using Agent for a one-line tweak is wasteful, using Ask for a refactor is frustrating.

tips.title: Reference files with #file and selections with #selection
tips.description: Use #file:path/to/file to scope chat to specific files, and #selection to focus on what you have highlighted. Both are far more precise than dumping entire folders into context.

tips.title: Let #codebase find the right context for you
tips.description: #codebase is now a single semantic, auto-managed index. Use it for project-wide questions and let the index pick the relevant files. Stop manually #file-listing five files when #codebase will route correctly.

tips.title: Attach images and video to chat
tips.description: Image attachments are mature; video attachments shipped April 2026. Paste a screenshot of an error dialog or a short screen recording of a UI bug instead of typing a long description.

tips.title: Use slash commands as polished prompts
tips.description: /explain, /fix, /tests, /doc, /new, /newNotebook, /troubleshoot, /optimize, /vulnerability — each one is a tuned prompt for a specific task. Reach for the slash command before you write a free-form chat.

tips.title: Address chat participants with @
tips.description: @workspace knows your project, @terminal knows your shell, @vscode knows the editor, @github knows the repo. Pick the right participant — answers from @workspace about a terminal command will be uselessly generic.

tips.title: Start a fresh thread when topics shift
tips.description: Click + to open a new chat thread when you switch tasks. Long, drifting threads bleed irrelevant context into every new question. Threads are free; use them.

tips.title: Enable Next Edit Suggestions for flow state
tips.description: Set github.copilot.nextEditSuggestions.enabled to true. NES predicts the next edit based on what you just changed and lets you Tab-jump through related edits across the file.

tips.title: Let NES handle repetitive renames
tips.description: When you rename a variable or add a parameter to a function, NES picks up the pattern and proposes the same edit at every other site. Tab through them instead of grep-and-edit.

tips.title: Use long-distance NES across the file
tips.description: Long-distance NES (February 2026) suggests edits anywhere in the file — not just adjacent to your cursor. Tab navigates you to the predicted edit site automatically.

tips.title: Write descriptive function names
tips.description: Copilot reads names as intent. sortArrayOfObjectsByProperty produces better suggestions than foo. The same naming discipline that helps human readers helps the model — it is not magic, just signal.

tips.title: Add a top-of-file purpose comment
tips.description: A short header — 'Express + TypeScript REST API for the orders service; uses Postgres via Prisma' — anchors every completion in the file. Especially valuable for greenfield modules where there is little code yet.

tips.title: Break complex requests into focused prompts
tips.description: 'Build the whole feature' yields generic code. 'Define the TypeScript interface' then 'write the validation' then 'wire the route' yields tighter, reviewable code. Smaller scope, better output.

tips.title: Provide input/output examples in prompts
tips.description: Especially for data transforms, two or three concrete input/output examples beat a paragraph of description. The model grounds on the examples and infers the edge cases.

tips.title: Set imports before asking for code
tips.description: Import the libraries and versions you intend to use first. Copilot reads imports as version intent and avoids suggesting outdated APIs from older releases of the same package.

tips.title: Use comment-driven development
tips.description: Write the comment describing what the next block should do, then let inline suggestions generate the code. The comment doubles as documentation and as the prompt that produced the code below it.

tips.title: Accept with Tab, dismiss with Esc
tips.description: Tab accepts the current inline suggestion; Esc dismisses it. Internalize this pair and your editing rhythm tightens — most of an inline-completions session is just Tab/Esc/keep typing.

tips.title: Cycle alternative suggestions with Alt+]
tips.description: Alt+] (Option+] on macOS) advances to the next suggestion; Alt+[ goes back. Copilot often has multiple valid solutions worth a glance — the second option is sometimes the one you actually want.

tips.title: Trigger inline suggestions manually with Alt+\
tips.description: Alt+\ (Option+\ on macOS) forces a fresh suggestion when one does not appear. Useful after a long pause or when you are editing existing code rather than writing new code.

tips.title: Open inline chat with Ctrl+I
tips.description: Ctrl+I (Cmd+I on macOS) opens inline chat right in the file. Perfect for surgical edits, single-question Q&A, or quick refactors without leaving your cursor position.

tips.title: Use AI-powered rename with F2
tips.description: F2 invokes rename, and Copilot proposes names that match your codebase conventions. Beats picking a generic name and grep-replacing later.

tips.title: Open Agent mode with Ctrl+Shift+I
tips.description: Ctrl+Shift+I jumps straight into Agent mode in chat. Memorize the keystroke — Agent is the default mode for non-trivial work and the menu path is slow.

tips.title: Generate unit tests with /tests
tips.description: Select a function and run /tests. Copilot generates typical, edge, and error-case tests based on the signature and body. Then run them — and watch which ones fail to find the actual bugs.

tips.title: Auto-generate commit messages with the sparkle icon
tips.description: The sparkle icon in the commit message box analyzes staged changes and produces a Conventional Commits-shaped message. Edit if needed; do not just rubber-stamp it. The message is part of your audit trail.

tips.title: Document code with /doc
tips.description: Select code, run /doc, and get JSDoc, docstrings, or inline comments matching your project's style. Tighter and more accurate than asking 'add comments' in free-form chat.

tips.title: Explain unfamiliar code with /explain
tips.description: Select any code block, run /explain, and get a detailed walkthrough. Use it as a learning tool when reading legacy code, third-party libraries, or another team's module.

tips.title: Diagnose runtime issues with /troubleshoot
tips.description: /troubleshoot, added in VS Code 1.115/1.116, walks through likely causes for runtime errors, build failures, and configuration issues. Use it before you copy the stack trace into a search engine.

tips.title: Always review before accepting a suggestion
tips.description: Copilot can hallucinate. Read the code, consider security implications, check for license exposure on suggestions matching public code. The five seconds of review is the cheapest insurance you have.

tips.title: Block suggestions matching public code
tips.description: If your org cares about license exposure, enable the 'block suggestions matching public code' setting. It trades some completion volume for a clean license posture; for many enterprises that is the right trade.

tips.title: Run /vulnerability on suspicious code
tips.description: /vulnerability scans selected code for common security issues — injection, weak crypto, unsafe deserialization. Use it on legacy code you have just inherited and on AI-generated boilerplate before it hits a PR.

tips.title: Layer Copilot output with linting and code scanning
tips.description: Pair AI-generated code with deterministic tools: linters, code scanning, secret scanning, IP scanning. Copilot is fast; static analysis is rigorous; together they catch what either misses alone.

tips.title: Use content exclusions for sensitive paths
tips.description: Content exclusions stop Copilot from reading or suggesting from configured paths. Use it for vendored secrets, generated code, and private data fixtures you do not want appearing in completions.

tips.title: Track adoption with the Copilot Metrics API
tips.description: Copilot Metrics went GA February 27, 2026 — dashboards and API are production-ready. Pull active users, acceptance rates, lines suggested, and time saved. Plan-mode telemetry reports as chat_panel_plan_mode separately.

tips.title: Track AI Credit burn per user and per repository
tips.description: The usage metrics API reports AI Credits consumed per user, and repository-level metrics went GA July 17, 2026. Pull both, find the heaviest repositories and models, then set per-user budgets and cost centers.

tips.title: Set custom enterprise roles for metrics access
tips.description: Enterprise Cloud supports custom roles for fine-grained metrics dashboard access. Give finance and engineering managers the views they need without handing out full admin.

tips.title: Build full-stack apps with GitHub Spark
tips.description: GitHub Spark (Pro+ and Enterprise) turns natural language into a full-stack app with live preview, embedded AI features, and a one-click open-in-VS-Code-agent or push-to-repo. Useful for rapid prototyping and demos.

tips.title: MCP servers replaced Copilot Extensions
tips.description: GitHub App-based Copilot Extensions were disabled on November 10, 2025. MCP servers replaced them: build once and the server works across any compatible agent. Tools are invoked with # in the IDE and can be called autonomously by agent mode and the cloud agent. Browse the MCP Registry in VS Code to install one.

tips.title: Stay current with the GitHub Changelog
tips.description: Copilot ships fast — a meaningful update lands roughly every two weeks. Subscribe to the GitHub Changelog and the GitHub Blog and skim them weekly; outdated practices are the most common cause of bad Copilot results.
